required.title: [Read Aloud / Read Together] Continued daily reading from high-quality literature
[Reference] A quality dictionary appropriate for the child's age
[For Mentor] Word Roots: Beginning
required.author: or similar etymology workbook for young learners

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] Aetosaur and Other Awesome Word Origins
suggested.author: or similar word history books for children

competence_markers.description: The child can identify and explain at least five common Latin or Greek roots and give example words for each
The child can break unfamiliar words into prefix, root, and suffix and make reasonable guesses about meaning
The child can use a dictionary independently to find word definitions, pronunciations, and etymologies
The child can use a thesaurus to find alternatives and choose the most precise option
The child demonstrates deliberate word choice in narrations — selecting specific, vivid words rather than defaulting to generic ones
The child can introduce themselves, ask basic questions, and express basic needs in their target language
The child can produce the Big Five in a second language with acceptable pronunciation
The child can explain in simple terms why knowing word origins helps with learning new words

narration_prompts.prompt: What is a root? Pick one root you have learned and tell me as many words as you can that come from it.
narration_prompts.detail: How does knowing the root help you understand the words?

narration_prompts.prompt: Take this word apart for me: [give an unfamiliar word built from known roots].
narration_prompts.detail: What are its pieces? What do the pieces mean? What do you think the whole word means?

narration_prompts.prompt: Look up a word in your dictionary and tell me everything you find.
narration_prompts.detail: The definition, the pronunciation, and if it is there, where the word came from.

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me five different ways to say "walk."
narration_prompts.detail: How is each one different? When would you use each one?

narration_prompts.prompt: Introduce yourself in your target language. Ask me my name. Say goodbye.
narration_prompts.detail: Then say hello and thank you in your second language.

narration_prompts.prompt: Why is it useful to know where words come from?
narration_prompts.detail: How does it help you learn new words faster?

cross_connections.description: Understanding how prefixes and suffixes change a word's function (noun to verb, verb to adjective) reinforces the structural awareness from 3.1.1. The child who knows that -tion turns a verb into a noun ("act" to "action") is learning morphology — one of the core concepts in 3.1.1.

Etymology connects directly to language families. The child who learns that "aqua" is Latin and "hydro" is Greek is learning that different languages contribute different vocabulary to English — and that these languages belong to the same Indo-European family discussed in 3.1.4.

Latin comes from Rome. Greek comes from Greece. The child studying etymology is encountering the civilizations that produced these languages — the same civilizations studied in 1.1.1. When the child learns that "aqueduct" comes from Latin "aqua" (water) + "ductus" (leading), they can connect this to the Roman aqueducts they learned about in history.

Scientific vocabulary is overwhelmingly Greek and Latin in origin. The child who knows Greek roots like "bio" (life), "geo" (earth), "hydro" (water), and "thermo" (heat) has a head start on every science term they will ever encounter.

Deliberate word choice is a writing skill as much as a vocabulary skill. The child who can choose between "ran," "sprinted," "bounded," and "crept" writes more vivid narrations. 3.1.3-A2's precision work directly feeds 3.2.1's composition skills.
Learning numbers in other languages connects vocabulary to numeracy. The child who can count to twenty in Spanish or Mandarin is building both linguistic and numerical fluency simultaneously.

content.why_this_matters:
In the last packet, you woke up to words. You started noticing them, collecting them, and using them with more precision. Now you are going to learn something that will make you a faster, smarter word-learner for the rest of your life: **where words come from.**

Most English words did not start out as English. They were borrowed, stolen, inherited, or adapted from other languages — languages that are hundreds or thousands of years old. The two most important source languages for English vocabulary are **Latin** and **Greek**. If you learn even a small number of Latin and Greek word parts — roots, prefixes, and suffixes — you gain the ability to figure out the meaning of thousands of words you have never seen before.

Here is an example. The Latin root **aqua** means "water." If you know that one root, you can figure out:
- **Aquarium** — a container for water (where fish live)
- **Aquatic** — having to do with water
- **Aqueduct** — a structure that carries water from one place to another

One root. Three words understood. And there are dozens more words built on **aqua** that you will encounter as you grow older. Every root you learn is a key that unlocks not one word but a whole family of words.

This packet also pushes your precision further. In A1, you learned to choose specific words over vague ones. Now you are going to learn to choose words **deliberately** — understanding not just what a word means but how it feels, what it suggests, and when it is the right word for the moment. You are going to learn to use a dictionary and a thesaurus as real tools, not just things that sit on a shelf. And you are going to expand your survival vocabulary in other languages, moving beyond the Big Five into functional phrases that let you do real things in the world.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Common Latin and Greek roots** — the building blocks that unlock thousands of English words
2. **Prefixes and suffixes** — the pieces added to roots that modify meaning (un-, re-, pre-, -tion, -able, -ful)
3. **Word families** — the ability to see connected words as branches of the same tree
4. **Dictionary and thesaurus skills** — using reference tools independently
5. **Deliberate word choice** — selecting words not just for meaning but for precision, tone, and effect
6. **Expanded survival vocabulary** — moving beyond the Big Five to functional phrases in the target language
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Where English Words Come From

English is a language that borrows from everywhere. Over its history, English has absorbed words from Latin, Greek, French, German, Old Norse, Arabic, Hindi, Nahuatl, Japanese, and dozens of other languages. But the two biggest sources of English vocabulary are:

**Latin** — the language of ancient Rome. When the Roman Empire controlled most of Europe, Latin spread everywhere. Even after the empire fell, Latin remained the language of the Church, of law, of science, and of educated people for over a thousand years. Roughly 60% of English words have Latin origins — either directly or through French (which is itself descended from Latin).

**Greek** — the language of ancient Greece. Greek gave English most of its scientific and medical vocabulary. When scientists needed a name for something new — a new animal, a new disease, a new idea — they usually built the name from Greek roots. That is why so many science words sound similar: they are built from the same Greek building blocks.

Understanding Latin and Greek roots is like having a secret decoder ring for English. Instead of learning words one at a time, you learn the pieces that words are built from — and suddenly you can decode words you have never encountered.

### Part 2: Roots, Prefixes, and Suffixes

Words are often built from parts, just like buildings are built from bricks. The three main parts are:

**Root** — the core meaning of a word. It is the part that carries the main idea.
- **aud** (Latin) = hear. Audio, audience, auditorium — all about hearing.
- **vis/vid** (Latin) = see. Vision, video, visible, evidence — all about seeing.
- **graph** (Greek) = write. Graphic, biography, autograph — all about writing.

**Prefix** — a piece added to the beginning of a word that changes its meaning.
- **un-** = not. Happy becomes unhappy. Known becomes unknown. Fair becomes unfair.
- **re-** = again. Build becomes rebuild. Play becomes replay. Write becomes rewrite.
- **pre-** = before. View becomes preview. Heat becomes preheat. School becomes preschool.

**Suffix** — a piece added to the end of a word that changes its meaning or its job in a sentence.
- **-able/-ible** = can be done. Wash becomes washable. Break becomes breakable. Eat becomes edible.
- **-tion/-sion** = the act of. Act becomes action. Decide becomes decision. Invent becomes invention.
- **-ful** = full of. Hope becomes hopeful. Joy becomes joyful. Care becomes careful.

When you can recognize these parts, you can break any unfamiliar word into pieces and figure out what it probably means.

**Example:** You encounter the word **invisible.**
- **in-** = not (a prefix meaning "not")
- **vis** = see (the Latin root for seeing)
- **-ible** = can be (a suffix meaning "able to be")
- Put it together: **not + see + able to be** = cannot be seen. Invisible.

**Example:** You encounter the word **submarine.**
- **sub-** = under (a Latin prefix)
- **mar** = sea (a Latin root — think "marine," "maritime")
- **-ine** = relating to (a suffix)
- Put it together: **under + sea + relating to** = something that goes under the sea. A submarine.

### Part 3: Ten Roots That Unlock Hundreds of Words

Here are ten of the most useful Latin and Greek roots. Each one appears in dozens of English words.

| Root | Origin | Meaning | Example Words |
|------|--------|---------|---------------|
| **aqua** | Latin | water | aquarium, aquatic, aqueduct |
| **aud** | Latin | hear | audio, audience, auditorium |
| **dict** | Latin | say, speak | dictionary, predict, verdict |
| **graph/gram** | Greek | write, draw | autograph, diagram, telegram |
| **port** | Latin | carry | transport, portable, export |
| **rupt** | Latin | break | erupt, interrupt, corrupt |
| **scrib/script** | Latin | write | describe, prescription, manuscript |
| **spec/spect** | Latin | look, see | inspect, spectacle, respect |
| **struct** | Latin | build | construct, structure, instruct |
| **terra** | Latin | earth, land | terrain, territory, terrace |

You do not need to memorize this chart. You need to recognize these roots when you see them in real words, and use them to figure out what unfamiliar words mean. Over the next several weeks, you will encounter each root multiple times in your reading — and each time, the connection will become stronger.

### Part 4: Word Families — Seeing the Branches

When you know a root, you can see its word family — all the words that grew from the same root, like branches growing from the same trunk.

Take the root **struct** (build):
- **Structure** — something that has been built
- **Construct** — to build something (con- = together)
- **Instruct** — to build knowledge in someone's mind (in- = in, into)
- **Destruct** — to un-build, to tear apart (de- = down, apart)
- **Obstruct** — to build something in the way, to block (ob- = against)
- **Infrastructure** — the built systems underneath everything else (infra- = below)

One root, six words — and each prefix changes the meaning in a logical way. This is not memorization. This is understanding a system.

Now take the root **port** (carry):
- **Transport** — to carry across (trans- = across)
- **Export** — to carry out (ex- = out)
- **Import** — to carry in (im- = in)
- **Portable** — able to be carried
- **Report** — to carry back (re- = back) — originally meant to bring information back
- **Support** — to carry from below (sub- = under, below)

Every time you learn a new prefix, every root you already know generates a new word. Every time you learn a new root, every prefix you already know generates a new word. The growth is not addition — it is multiplication.

### Part 5: Using a Dictionary and Thesaurus

A dictionary is not a book you read from cover to cover. It is a tool you reach for when you need it — like a hammer or a wrench.

**When to use a dictionary:**
- When you encounter a word you do not know and context clues are not enough
- When you think you know what a word means but want to be sure
- When you want to know how to pronounce a word correctly
- When you want to know where a word came from (many dictionaries include etymology)

**How to use a dictionary:** Words are arranged in alphabetical order. Guide words at the top of each page show the first and last word on that page. Find the right section, then scan for your word. Read the definition. Read the example sentence. If there is an etymology note, read that too — it will help you remember the word.

**A thesaurus** is a different kind of tool. It does not define words — it gives you alternatives. When you know what you want to say but the word you have is not quite right, a thesaurus shows you other options.

If you look up "said" in a thesaurus, you might find: stated, declared, announced, whispered, muttered, exclaimed, replied, responded, mentioned, noted. Each one means something slightly different. "Whispered" is not the same as "exclaimed." The thesaurus gives you the options; you choose the right one.

**The danger of a thesaurus:** Never use a thesaurus word you do not understand. If you look up "happy" and find "ebullient," do not use "ebullient" unless you know what it means and are sure it fits. Using big words incorrectly is worse than using simple words correctly. The thesaurus is a menu of options, not a substitute for understanding.

### Part 6: Deliberate Word Choice — Saying Exactly What You Mean

Every time you speak or write, you make choices about which words to use. Most of the time, you make these choices automatically — you grab the first word that comes to mind. But the most powerful communicators choose their words deliberately.

Consider the difference:
- "The dog **ran** across the yard." (Fine. Clear.)
- "The dog **sprinted** across the yard." (The dog was running fast, with urgency.)
- "The dog **bounded** across the yard." (The dog was running with big, joyful leaps.)
- "The dog **scrambled** across the yard." (The dog was running clumsily, maybe on slippery ground.)
- "The dog **crept** across the yard." (The dog was moving slowly and carefully — not running at all.)

Same sentence structure. Different verb. Completely different picture in your mind.

This is what deliberate word choice means: instead of reaching for the first word that sort of works, you pause and ask, "What is the **exact** right word for what I mean?" This takes practice. It takes knowing many words. And it takes the willingness to think for a moment before speaking or writing.

You will not do this with every sentence — that would be exhausting. But the habit of sometimes pausing to find the right word, especially when you are writing or when precision matters, is one of the most valuable language skills you can develop.

### Part 7: Expanding Your Survival Vocabulary

In A1, you learned the Big Five (hello, please, thank you, yes, no) and basic survival words in your target language. Now you are going to expand to functional phrases — groups of words that let you do real things.

**Phrases you are adding:**
- "My name is..." and "What is your name?"
- "Where is the...?" (bathroom, exit, hospital)
- "How much does this cost?"
- "I don't understand. Can you speak slowly?"
- "I am hungry" and "I am thirsty"
- "Excuse me" and "I am sorry"
- Numbers from one to twenty (and eventually to one hundred)

Notice that these are not vocabulary lists organized by grammar category. They are organized by **what you need to do.** You need to introduce yourself, find things, buy things, ask for help, and express basic needs. That is the principle behind the Context-Based 150 system you will learn fully in Weathering Storms: vocabulary organized by function, not by category.

You are also going to start learning the Big Five in a **second** language. Not deep study — just hello, please, thank you, yes, no. Two languages of survival words means two more groups of people you can show respect to and begin communicating with.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: Root of the Week [Ongoing — Backbone of the Packet]
Each week, introduce one Latin or Greek root from the list in Part 3 (or beyond it). Write the root, its meaning, and its origin on a card and post it on the wall. Throughout the week, both mentor and student look for words containing that root — in books, in conversation, on signs. Each discovery gets added to the card. By the end of the packet, the wall displays a map of word families.

### Study 2: Word Detective — Breaking Words Apart [Early to Mid-Packet]
Give the child unfamiliar words built from known roots and prefixes. Let them break the word into parts and guess the meaning before looking it up. Start easy: "unhappy" (un + happy), "replay" (re + play). Build to harder: "invisible" (in + vis + ible), "transport" (trans + port). Celebrate correct guesses and discuss near-misses.

### Study 3: Dictionary Practice [Mid-Packet]
The child practices finding words in a physical dictionary. Time it — not competitively, but to build fluency with alphabetical navigation. Once the child can find a word within a minute, they are ready to use the dictionary as a real tool rather than a frustrating obstacle. Practice looking up words from current reading.

### Study 4: The Verb Precision Exercise [Mid-Packet]
Give the child a simple sentence with a generic verb ("The cat went across the room") and ask them to replace the verb with five more precise alternatives. Discuss which alternative best fits different scenarios. This can be done orally or in writing. Repeat with different sentences until the child begins doing it spontaneously.

### Study 5: Functional Phrases in the Target Language [Mid-to-Late Packet]
Expand from the Big Five to functional phrases. Practice introductions, simple questions, and expressions of need. Role-play scenarios: "You are at a market in [country]. How do you ask how much something costs? How do you say thank you when you buy it?" If possible, practice with native speakers.

### Study 6: Big Five in a Second Language [Late Packet]
Introduce the Big Five in a second language — ideally one from a different language family than the first. A child whose target language is Spanish (Romance/Latin) might learn the Big Five in Mandarin or Arabic. The point is not mastery — it is the experience of encountering a very different sound system and the confidence that comes from being willing to try.

### Study 7: Word Family Tree [End of Packet]
The child chooses one root they have studied and creates a visual "word family tree" — the root as the trunk, each word as a branch, with the prefix or suffix that creates each word labeled. This can be drawn, painted, or built as a craft project. Display it as a record of what etymology makes visible: words are not random collections of letters — they are structured, logical, and connected.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Root Recognition
**Developing well:** The child spots Latin and Greek roots in unfamiliar words without prompting. They make connections: "Is 'spectator' from 'spec' — like someone who watches?" They use root knowledge to make educated guesses about new words.

**Developing slowly:** The child can recite roots from memory but does not recognize them in real words. Root knowledge stays abstract rather than applied.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you introducing roots in context — in real words from real reading — or as an isolated exercise? The child who learns "aud = hear" by being shown "audience, auditorium, audio" in books they are reading learns it differently than the child who sees it on a flashcard.

### 2. Word-Part Analysis
**Developing well:** Given an unfamiliar word, the child attempts to break it into parts and reason about the meaning. Their analyses are logical even when not perfectly accurate. They understand the system (prefix + root + suffix) and can apply it.

**Developing slowly:** The child cannot break words into parts, or breaks them at random places that do not correspond to actual morphemes. They treat word analysis as guessing rather than systematic reasoning.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you practiced the breakdown process explicitly — showing the child how to identify the root first, then the prefix, then the suffix? Have you done enough examples that the process is becoming familiar?

### 3. Dictionary and Thesaurus Competence
**Developing well:** The child can find a word in a physical dictionary within about a minute. They read the full entry — definition, pronunciation, and etymology if available. They use the thesaurus to find alternatives and can explain which alternative fits best.

**Developing slowly:** The child is still lost in alphabetical navigation. They read only the first definition and miss nuance. They use thesaurus words without understanding them.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you practiced enough? Dictionary skills are motor skills as much as intellectual ones — they require repeated practice to become fluent. Have you modeled careful reading of full entries?

### 4. Deliberate Word Choice
**Developing well:** The child sometimes pauses in speech or writing to search for a more precise word. Their narrations include vivid, specific language at least occasionally. They can generate multiple alternatives for a generic word when prompted.

**Developing slowly:** The child's language remains entirely generic. When asked for a more precise word, they cannot generate alternatives.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are they reading rich enough material? Precise language is absorbed from exposure. A child who reads only simple texts has no models for vivid word choice. Are you modeling deliberate word choice yourself?

### 5. Expanded Survival Vocabulary
**Developing well:** The child can introduce themselves, ask simple questions, and express basic needs in their target language. Pronunciation is intelligible. They can also produce the Big Five in a second language.

**Developing slowly:** The child cannot produce functional phrases even with prompting. Pronunciation remains unintelligible.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is practice happening daily? Even two minutes of daily practice builds more than twenty minutes weekly. Is the child hearing native-speaker pronunciation through audio or live interaction?
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Making Etymology a Drill
If you present roots as flashcards to memorize and quiz, you will miss the point. Etymology is fascinating when it is encountered as discovery — "Oh, that word comes from the Latin for 'to carry'!" It is deadening when encountered as a memorization requirement. Keep it alive. Keep it in context. Keep it connected to real reading.

### Over-Correcting Word Choice
The child who is told "that's too vague" every time they speak will stop speaking. Precision prompts should be occasional, gentle, and framed as expansion ("What if we said 'towering' instead of 'big'? Can you hear the difference?") rather than correction ("Don't say 'big.'").

### Skipping the Physical Dictionary
Digital tools are convenient, but a physical dictionary teaches skills that a search bar does not: alphabetical navigation, scanning, reading entries in context, discovering nearby words you were not looking for. The child should have a relationship with a physical dictionary at this age. Digital tools can supplement later.

### Treating the Target Language as an Add-On
If survival vocabulary practice happens only when you remember to fit it in, it will not stick. Build it into the daily routine — even a brief daily check-in ("How do you say 'excuse me' in [language]?") maintains the knowledge. If it is not routine, it is not real.
